html:
GAF Commercial Roof Systems and Where They Fit in Lansing

GAF's commercial division covers a wide range of low-slope roofing categories, including TPO, EPDM, modified bitumen, and built-up systems, along with coatings for restoration work. That breadth is useful on a market like Lansing where the building stock spans everything from newer retail construction to plant-era manufacturing buildings still running on decades-old built-up or modified bitumen roofs.

Product Breadth Matters on a Mixed-Age Building Stock

Not every roof in this market is a candidate for the same membrane category. A newer office building near Okemos might be a straightforward TPO project, while a manufacturing building around Grand Ledge or Charlotte with an existing built-up roof might be better served by a modified bitumen recover that respects the original multi-ply assembly rather than switching membrane families entirely. Having a manufacturer with product options across both categories keeps the specification decision focused on what the roof needs rather than what one narrow product line can offer.

We treat the manufacturer's breadth as a tool, not a reason to pick one system over another by default. The building and its condition still drive the actual recommendation.

That approach matters most on buildings that have grown over time through additions, since a single campus or facility can end up with two or three different roof ages and construction types under one address, each one needing its own evaluation rather than a blanket recommendation.

Modified Bitumen and Built-Up Options for Legacy Roofs

Buildings with an existing modified bitumen or built-up roof often make more sense as a recover within the same general system family than as a full conversion to single-ply, particularly when the existing base plies are sound and dry. GAF's modified bitumen and built-up product lines give us that option on plant-era buildings where tearing off a multi-ply system down to bare deck would add cost and disruption without a clear performance benefit over a properly executed recover.

This matters on buildings like the manufacturing facilities near the I-96 industrial corridor and older commercial buildings in Eaton Rapids, where the existing roof structure and detailing already reflect decades of built-up roofing practice.

Switching a legacy multi-ply roof to single-ply isn't wrong on every project, but it usually means a full tear-off, new insulation, and new detailing at every penetration, which adds cost that a compatible recover avoids when the existing plies are still in good condition.

Cold-Weather Application Considerations Across Categories

Each product category GAF offers has its own temperature and weather constraints for proper installation. TPO seams need workable hot-air welding temperatures, modified bitumen application, whether torch-applied or cold-applied, has its own set of conditions, and coatings need dry, stable weather to cure. We plan project timing around whichever category is actually being installed rather than applying a single generic weather rule across every job.

Michigan's shift from a wet fall into hard freeze narrows every one of these windows to some degree, which is why we build schedule buffer into fall projects regardless of which product category is going on the roof.

What We Evaluate Before Recommending a Category

The same short list of factors comes up on almost every project, regardless of whether the roof ends up as single-ply or a multi-ply recover:

  • Whether the existing roof is single-ply, modified bitumen, or built-up
  • Deck condition and whether it supports a recover or needs full tear-off
  • Building occupancy and how disruptive a tear-off would be
  • Drainage and ponding history on the existing roof
  • Budget relative to the building's expected remaining service life
  • Whether reflectivity or energy performance is a stated priority

Those factors point us toward a product category first, and only then toward the specific system within that category that fits the building.

Restoration Coatings as a Middle Option

For roofs that aren't quite ready for full replacement but need more than a patch, coating products offer a middle path that extends service life without committing to a full recover or tear-off. We evaluate coating candidacy the same way regardless of manufacturer: dry substrate, sound structure, and enough remaining membrane flexibility to bond properly.

We'll recommend this path honestly when it fits and steer owners toward recover or replacement instead when a roof's condition means coating would only delay an inevitable, more expensive project down the line. That candor matters most on public and institutional buildings where a wasted coating expense is harder to justify at the next budget review than it might be for a privately held commercial property.

Questions Owners Ask About GAF Commercial Systems

Does GAF only make residential shingle products?

No. GAF's commercial division covers low-slope systems including TPO, EPDM, modified bitumen, built-up roofing, and coatings, separate from their residential steep-slope products.

Is modified bitumen an outdated system compared to single-ply?

Not outdated, just different. Multi-ply systems offer redundancy that can suit certain buildings well, particularly where matching an existing assembly makes more sense than converting to single-ply.

Can a built-up roof be recovered instead of torn off?

Often, if the existing plies are dry and sound. We confirm that with a moisture survey before recommending a recover over any existing multi-ply roof.

How do we choose between TPO and modified bitumen for our building?

We look at the existing roof system, deck condition, and how the building is used, then recommend the category that fits rather than defaulting to whichever is currently more common.

Does product category affect long-term maintenance needs?

Yes. Multi-ply systems and single-ply membranes have different maintenance patterns, and we walk owners through what to expect for whichever category ends up on their roof.
